steps.title: Spot the cause-and-effect structure
steps.content: The passage first explains Precisionist values (cold, machine-like perfection, geometric shapes), then describes what subjects they actually painted. The third sentence presents what naturally results from the second.

steps.title: Match the transition to consequential logic
steps.content:
The logical relationship is cause-and-effect: because Precisionists valued geometric shapes and machine-like perfection, they naturally painted subjects like skyscrapers, bridges, and factories that embody those qualities. The blank needs a transition signaling that the subject matter follows logically from the aesthetic values.
  • Choice A — "However,": signals contrast, so fails — the subjects Precisionists chose are not surprising or contradictory given their values; they fit perfectly. ✗
  • Choice B — "That said,": signals a qualifying concession, so fails — the third sentence does not walk back or limit what sentence two stated. ✗
  • Choice C — "Accordingly,": signals a result that follows logically, so fits — skyscrapers and factories are the expected subjects for artists who prize geometric, machine-like perfection. ✓
  • Choice D — "In the end,": signals a final conclusion after a process, so fails — no sequence of events is being resolved here. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
